institutional_history.description: Malaysia Agreement 1963 (MA63) merges Federation of Malaya, Singapore (until 1965), Sabah, and Sarawak. Twenty Points (Sabah) and Eighteen Points (Sarawak) form the constitutional bargain. Article 161A defines 'native' status; Article 95B-95E covers state-list adjustments for Sabah/Sarawak.

Constitution (Amendment) Act 1976 reclassifies Sabah and Sarawak from 'States' under Article 1(2) to one of '13 states', textually flattening the federal structure agreed in MA63. The amendment is the single most-disputed institutional move from the Borneo side.

Constitutional Amendments restore the 'pursuant to Malaysia Agreement' language and re-establish Sabah/Sarawak as 'territories of the Federation' as before 1976. State-rights revival ongoing but incomplete. MA63 implementation committee meets sporadically.

GRS-GPS parliamentary bloc (49 MPs) demands Ministry of Borneo Affairs (T4A issue 1705). Sabah election Nov 2025 returns GRS with 'Sabah First' narrative outperforming federal-affiliation appeals (T4A issue 1701).

primary_sources.title: Malaysia Agreement 1963 (UK Treaty Series); Inter-Governmental Committee Report 1962

notes: The Peninsular-Borneo cleavage is institutionally produced via the asymmetric federalism designed in MA63 and partly dismantled in 1976. Sabah and Sarawak each have their own institutional categories of 'natives' that are partially distinct from Peninsular Bumiputera. Resource-revenue arrangements (5% oil royalty) are a recurring grievance feeding the cleavage. Borneo state elections operate on a separate election cycle from federal/Peninsular states.
